slide_1_Cover: AI Decision Intelligence for Distribution & Logistics
slide_2_Idea_Problem_Solution_Value: Only solution learning SKU-level patterns and workflows together — generic ERP modules cannot match depth of AI decision intelligence built from company operations
Non-invasive layer on existing systems with zero migration — deploys in days with near-zero switching cost, minimal organizational disruption, and low implementation risk
Land-and-expand model starts with single workflow, expands to full purchasing cycle, increasing ACV per account through multi-workflow and multi-site expansion over time
SKU-specific decision intelligence learning item-level patterns and company workflows to deliver context-aware purchasing recommendations instead of generic advice
Approval-based execution connects AI recommendations directly to real purchasing workflows — governance through decision traceability, not just dashboards and reports
Data normalization engine structures fragmented ERP/WMS/OMS data into a usable decision layer in real time — no manual preprocessing needed by customer teams
Teams use spreadsheets for purchasing even with ERP/WMS in place — decision-making stays manual, experience-based, not data-driven or repeatable
Purchasing logic varies by SKU, supplier, and process; no standardized system exists to encode decision-making rules at the item level across the organization and context
Sales, inventory, lead time data scattered across multiple systems — days of manual consolidation and data cleanup required before any purchasing decision can be made
slide_3_Market_1_3_TAM_SAM_SOM_+_Why_Now: 98K exporting SMEs at record high in 2025; ERP/WMS adopted but purchasing decisions run on Excel — clear opportunity for AI decision layer insertion
McKinsey/BCG/Bain cite AI supply chain planning as top-priority application; online commerce grew 13.3% YoY in Korea driving demand for automated purchasing
Netstock/Lokad/RELEX prove mid-market decision layer category exists globally; SCM market grows from $25.7B to $48.6B by 2030 with accelerating AI adoption
Global SCM software market (Grand View Research verified)
Y1-Y3 recurring revenue from base to optimistic scenario
Grand View Research: global SCM software $25.67B in 2024, projected $48.59B by 2030 — Tier 1
Includes SAP/Oracle/Blue Yonder/RELEX/o9; VANDRO targets decision layer within this market
Korean SMEs with ERP but spreadsheet-based purchasing decisions; upper segment willing to buy
15 customers x $14K pilot + 60% convert to $29K = $471K; optimistic: 40 x $36K
Founder target 50 x $43K = $2.1M is upper bound; actual depends on conversion rates
slide_4_Market_2_3_Competition: Korea top ERP with EQT $930M investment; owns customer data and UI infrastructure — most realistic strategic threat if AI added
SKU-aware planning at scale with $5B valuation; strong but enterprise focus means heavy implementation and long sales cycles
Mid-market inventory planning on existing ERP — closest commercial benchmark but no APAC presence or regional localization
Strongest non-price competitor; low cost but high people-dependency, no governance trail, no decision audit capabilities
Targets gap between heavy enterprise suites (SAP/Oracle) and manual Excel — lighter, faster implementation tailored for mid-market with lower total cost of ownership
Data readiness built into product core — works with messy fragmented data that RELEX/o9/Netstock require pre-cleaned and structured by customers before use
Approval-based execution connects AI to real purchasing workflows with governance and traceability — decision-to-action workflows with audit trails, not dashboards
slide_5_Market_3_3_GTM_Pricing_BM_Scale: Implementation $36K + annual subscription $68K = $95-104K first-year ACV per customer
Progressive ACV expansion as workflows and sites added across customer account base
Value anchor: reduced stockouts, overstock, faster decision cycles and margin improvement
Hybrid B2B SaaS: implementation fee for deployment plus recurring subscription; 5 customers at $5.7K/month each, zero churn
Land-and-expand: single workflow entry, then upsell across purchasing cycle and sites — MRR grew 150% in one quarter
Stage 1: Korea F&B, franchise, 3PL via founder-led sales and paid pilots — 5 paying customers acquired through direct outreach and reference compounding
Stage 2: Land-and-expand within Korea via workflow expansion, multi-site rollouts, and ERP/WMS implementation partner co-sell for new account referrals
Stage 3: GMEP-backed Europe expansion into Germany and Finland plus Latin America via Colombia PoC — channel partnerships and localization at scale
High ACV land-and-expand model: single workflow entry with multi-site and multi-workflow expansion accelerating revenue per customer and account lifetime value
Layers on existing ERP/WMS systems — adoption friction minimal, implementation repeatable and templatizable across F&B, franchise, 3PL verticals and geographies
Data readiness built into product architecture — works with messy fragmented data sources, no customer pre-cleaning required, accelerating time-to-value

Previous startup collaboration proved complementary skill fit across business, product, and engineering functions before co-founding VANDRO — working relationship fully validated
Role clarity: CEO drives GTM, sales, fundraising, customer relationships; CTO leads AI engine development, data pipeline architecture, and product technical direction overall

Two additional engineers joined after the product direction was set: Younggyun Cho (full-stack, ex-Naver) leads backend and data integration, and Taesic Choi (frontend) builds the dashboard and workflow UI
Second-time founder with prior CMO experience and go-to-market cycle leadership

Former startup AI engineer with hands-on AI deployment and scaling experience

Ex-engineer at Korea Electric Power Corporation
slide_7_Team_2_4_Program_Trajectory: Jeonghyeon (CEO) and Minwoo (CTO) joined Antler as preformed team with demonstrated prior startup collaboration and complementary skill fit
Co-founded VANDRO before Antler from proven prior startup working relationship — validated collaboration history and mutual trust between founders
Team debated broad AI vs SKU-level focus; CTO challenged assumptions constructively throughout bootcamp, refining product thesis and market positioning strategy

Bootcamp 1 conditional approval from Gabriel at score 7; advised team to raise ambition on positioning and market expansion scope significantly
Office Hour score 8 from Gabriel; execution momentum positive but Gabriel advised improved communication structure and investor pitch clarity
5 paying customers at $28.5K MRR ($340K ARR) with zero churn — GMEP selected for Germany and Finland expansion, $164K hardware contract closing
Summarizes the journey from program start through team formation and PMF validation, based on weekly founder retrospectives and program team observations
slide_8_Team_3_4_Evaluation: Prior startup collab, proven fit, 51:49 equity. Working relationship validates mutual trust and complementary skills
5 paying customers, $28.5K MRR ($340K ARR), zero churn, GMEP selected, $164K hardware contract
Narrowed from broad AI to SKU-level intelligence. Gradual adjustment based on customer feedback, not dramatic pivot

slide_9_Team_4_4_Strengths_&_Improvement: Deloitte/McKinsey/BCG/Bain confirm purchasing gap between ERP and operations is real, growing, structural
5 paying customers at $28.5K MRR ($340K ARR) with zero churn — recurring contracts not pilots, 150% MRR growth
Layers on existing ERP/WMS with zero migration — lowest adoption friction where switching costs block competitors
SAP/Oracle/RELEX/o9/Netstock/Lokad already sell decision layers on ERP — VANDRO late entrant
KRW 230M hardware contract and implementation fees blur SaaS metrics — pure subscription ARR unclear

slide_10_Investor_Assessment: While SOM seems to be moderate, the solution has strong potential to capture adjacent markets of WMS, OMS, TMS and small ERP as clues are shown from the teams current enterprise customers. All inclusive, the market is huge
Competition will become more fierce as LLMs and vertical agents become more advanced and popular. Integrated WMS, OMS, TMS providers can expand into where Vandro is playing
The team is building moat around vertical-specific, SKU-specific and company-specific data for enterprise-quality decision making processes

The team succeeded in building trust with and making existing customers request more functions and layers to be added on top of current product and pay more over time. Now the revenue retention is higher than 400% over the last 8 months
More opportunities in other countries, and the team is already getting interest and traction from overseas
Hustling, balanced team with domain expertise and business development skills

Overall Summary : Great team that grew fast during residency demonstrating their capabilities of delivering high-quality product to customers making them pay more fees over months. The team solves the real problem that most logistics companies with F&B inventories suffer from even with advanced WMS, OMS, TMS, and ERP as proven by their enterprise customers
Investment Decision Rationale : Growth trajectory in terms of revenue retention shows a good slope.

Key Risks : Team has strong bias towards execution so it needs a seasoned strategist to grow faster, communicate better internally and externally, and manage globally while delivering quality services.
